items.subtopic: Men's Health (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Sex-Specific Mortality Trends in Middle-Aged Men
items.content: Since 2010, there has been a concerning rise in mortality rates among young and middle-aged men in the United States. This increase is attributed to deaths from drug overdose, alcohol-related liver disease, and suicide, often referred to as 'deaths of despair.' These trends are particularly pronounced in regions such as the Ohio Valley, Appalachia, and certain New England states.
keyPoints: Mortality rates have risen for men aged 25-64 since 2010.
Life expectancy has decreased for young and middle-aged men during this period.
The highest increase in mortality is observed in the Ohio Valley, Appalachia, and New Hampshire, Maine, Vermont.
Rising 'deaths of despair' are linked to economic and social deterioration.
items.reference: Men s Health · Men's Health (part 1)

items.subtopic: Men's Health (part 1)
items.title: Disease Prevalence Differences Between Men and Women
items.content: Men have a higher prevalence of neurodevelopmental and degenerative disorders, substance use disorders, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, liver cirrhosis, and certain cancers like prostate, melanoma, and pancreatic cancer. Conversely, women are more prone to autoimmune disorders, depression, rheumatologic disorders, and osteoporosis.
keyPoints: Men have higher prevalence of neurodevelopmental and degenerative disorders.
Substance use disorders, including performance-enhancing drugs and alcohol use disorder, are more common in men.
Cardiovascular disease is prevalent among men aged 35-64.
Women have a higher prevalence of autoimmune disorders, depression, rheumatologic disorders, osteoporosis.
items.reference: Men s Health · Men's Health (part 1)

items.subtopic: Men's Health (part 1)
items.title: Leading Causes of Death in Men Across Different Age Groups
items.content: The leading causes of death vary by age group among men. For those aged 15-34, unintentional injuries, homicides, and suicides are predominant. In the 35-64 age bracket, heart disease, cancer, and unintentional injuries lead. Among men aged 65 and older, heart disease, cancer, lower respiratory tract infections, and stroke are the major causes.
keyPoints: Unintentional injuries, homicides, suicides account for over three-fourths of deaths in men aged 15-34.
Heart disease, cancer, unintentional injuries are leading causes of death for men aged 35-64.
For men ≥65 years, heart disease, cancer, lower respiratory tract infections, stroke are major causes.
items.reference: Men s Health · Men's Health (part 1)

items.subtopic: Men's Health (part 2)
items.title: Impact of Y Chromosome Loss with Aging in Men
items.content: The loss of the Y chromosome as men age is linked to increased risks for heart disease, particularly heart failure, certain cancers, and a shortened lifespan. This genetic change may affect somatic cell function due to altered expression of Y chromosome genes.
keyPoints: Loss of Y chromosome with aging in men
Increased risk of heart disease (especially heart failure)
Higher susceptibility to certain types of cancers
Shortened life span
items.reference: Men s Health · Men's Health (part 2)

items.subtopic: Men's Health (part 2)
items.title: Common Concerns Addressed in Men's Health Clinics
items.content: Men's health clinics primarily address sexual, reproductive, and urologic health issues. Common conditions treated include testosterone deficiency syndromes, age-related decline in testosterone levels, sexual dysfunction, muscle dysmorphia, anabolic-androgenic steroid use, lower urinary tract symptoms, and complications from prostate cancer therapy.
keyPoints: Testosterone deficiency syndromes
Age-related decline in testosterone levels
Sexual dysfunction
Muscle dysmorphia and AAS use
Lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS)
Medical complications of prostate cancer therapy
items.reference: Men s Health · Men's Health (part 2)

items.subtopic: AGING-RELATED CHANGES IN MALE REPRODUCTIVE FUNCTION
items.title: Testosterone Decline and Hypogonadism in Aging Men
items.content: Aging is associated with a decline in testosterone levels due to defects at all levels of the hypothalamic-pituitary-testicular (HPT) axis. This includes attenuated pulsatile gonadotropin-releasing hormone secretion, reduced luteinizing hormone response, and impaired Leydig cell function.
keyPoints: Testosterone concentrations decrease with advancing age starting in the third decade of life.
In older men, free testosterone declines more than total testosterone due to higher SHBG levels.
2.1% of community-dwelling men aged 40–70 years have total testosterone <317 ng/dL and free testosterone <64 pg/mL with sexual symptoms.

Low testosterone is linked to decreased sexual desire, poor erections, reduced muscle mass/strength, increased risk of falls, insulin resistance/type 2 diabetes, higher mortality rates, lower bone density, and depressive symptoms.
items.reference: Men s Health · AGING-RELATED CHANGES IN MALE REPRODUCTIVE FUNCTION

items.subtopic: AGING-RELATED CHANGES IN MALE REPRODUCTIVE FUNCTION
items.title: Genetic Influence on Testosterone Levels and Associated Risks
items.content: Mendelian randomization studies indicate a sexual dimorphic relationship between genetically determined testosterone levels and health risks, such as type 2 diabetes and prostate cancer.
keyPoints: Lower genetically determined testosterone levels in men are associated with higher risk of type 2 diabetes.
Higher genetically determined testosterone levels increase the risk of prostate cancer in men.
In women, higher genetically determined testosterone is linked to increased risk of type 2 diabetes.
items.reference: Men s Health · AGING-RELATED CHANGES IN MALE REPRODUCTIVE FUNCTION

items.subtopic: TABLE 411-1 Association of Testosterone Levels with Outcomes in Older Men | EFFICACY OF TESTOSTERONE REPLACEMENT THERAPY IN MIDDLE-AGED AND OLDER MEN WITH HYPOGONADISM
items.title: Association of Testosterone Levels with Health Outcomes in Older Men
items.content: Testosterone levels are positively associated with muscle mass, strength, physical function, sexual desire, and bone health. They are also negatively associated with risks of coronary artery disease, type 2 diabetes mellitus, metabolic syndrome, all-cause mortality, falls, fractures, dementia, Alzheimer's disease, frailty, and late-onset low-grade persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia). Testosterone levels do not show an association with lower urinary tract symptoms, erectile dysfunction, or major depressive disorder.
keyPoints: Positively associated with muscle mass, strength, physical function, sexual desire, bone health

Negatively associated with coronary artery disease, type 2 diabetes mellitus, metabolic syndrome, all-cause mortality, falls and fracture risk, dementia and Alzheimer's disease, frailty, late-onset low-grade persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia)
Not associated with lower urinary tract symptoms, erectile dysfunction, major depressive disorder
items.reference: Men s Health · TABLE 411-1 Association of Testosterone Levels with Outcomes in Older Men | EFFICACY OF TESTOSTERONE REPLACEMENT THERAPY IN MIDDLE-AGED AND OLDER MEN WITH HYPOGONADISM

items.subtopic: EFFICACY OF TESTOSTERONE REPLACEMENT THERAPY IN MIDDLE-AGED AND OLDER MEN WITH HYPOGONADISM
items.title: Efficacy and Safety of Testosterone Replacement Therapy in Older Men with Hypogonadism
items.content: Testosterone replacement therapy (TRT) has been evaluated through large trials like the TTrials and TRAVERSE. These studies show that TRT improves sexual activity, desire, and hypogonadal symptoms more effectively than placebo. It also helps correct anemia in men with low testosterone levels. However, its effects on erectile function are small and inconsistent. The TRAVERSE trial specifically assessed cardiovascular safety in men aged 45-80 years with preexisting CAD or increased risk of CAD.
keyPoints: TTrials enrolled 788 men aged ≥65 years with <275 ng/dL testosterone
TRAVERSE trial for men aged 45–80 years with <300 ng/dL testosterone and preexisting CAD or increased risk of CAD
TRT associated with greater improvement in sexual activity, desire, hypogonadal symptoms than placebo
Effects on erectile function small and inconsistent
Nearly 15% had anemia; TRT corrected more cases than placebo
50% reported depressive symptoms at baseline; only 1.5% met criteria for late-onset low-grade persistent depressive disorder
items.reference: Men s Health · TABLE 411-1 Association of Testosterone Levels with Outcomes in Older Men | EFFICACY OF TESTOSTERONE REPLACEMENT THERAPY IN MIDDLE-AGED AND OLDER MEN WITH HYPOGONADISM

items.subtopic: Strong Evidence of Efficacy | Suggestive Evidence of Efficacy
items.title: Testosterone Replacement Therapy in Hypogonadal Men: Improving Sexual and Physical Health
items.content: Testosterone replacement therapy (TRT) has strong evidence supporting its efficacy in improving overall sexual activity, desire, energy levels, mood, muscle mass, bone density, and anemia correction in hypogonadal men. The TTrials demonstrated significant benefits in sexual function, mobility, and vitality for older men with low testosterone levels.
keyPoints: Improves sexual activity and desire in men with low libido
Relieves hypogonadal symptoms
Improves depressive symptoms and energy level
Increases skeletal muscle mass and reduces fat mass
Corrects unexplained anemia
Enhances bone mineral density and strength
TTrials: Testosterone treatment for men aged 65+ with testosterone levels <275 ng/dL improved sexual function, mobility, vitality
TRAVERSE trial: Large sample size, longer duration; assessed cardiovascular events in men aged 45–80 years with testosterone <300 ng/dL and coronary artery disease risk
items.reference: Men s Health · Strong Evidence of Efficacy | Suggestive Evidence of Efficacy

items.subtopic: Strong Evidence of Efficacy | Suggestive Evidence of Efficacy
items.title: Testosterone Replacement Therapy: Safety Profile and Limitations
items.content: While TRT shows efficacy in several areas, it does not improve glycemic control in men with prediabetes or diabetes. The TRAVERSE trial indicated no significant difference in the progression from prediabetes to diabetes between testosterone- and placebo-treated groups.
keyPoints: No improvement in glycemic control for men with hypogonadism and prediabetes
TRAVERSE trial: Similar incidence of diabetes progression in testosterone vs. placebo groups
Testosterone treatment should not be used alone to manage diabetes or prediabetes
items.reference: Men s Health · Strong Evidence of Efficacy | Suggestive Evidence of Efficacy

items.subtopic: C | SAFETY OF TESTOSTERONE REPLACEMENT THERAPY IN MIDDLE-AGED AND OLDER MEN WITH HYPOGONADISM | Older Men with Age-Related Decline in Testosterone
items.title: When to Offer Testosterone Therapy for Hypogonadism in Older Men
items.content: Testosterone therapy should be considered on an individualized basis for men over 65 years with symptoms or conditions suggestive of testosterone deficiency, such as low libido, significant depressive symptoms, fatigue, or unexplained anemia. It is crucial to confirm testosterone deficiency through two or more early morning fasting levels below the lower limit of normal for healthy young men.
keyPoints: Testosterone therapy should be offered on an individualized basis in men >65 years with suggestive symptoms and consistently low testosterone.
Confirm testosterone deficiency with two or more early morning, fasting testosterone levels below the lower limit of normal for healthy young men.
Evaluate the burden of symptoms against potential benefits and risks.
items.reference: Men s Health · C | SAFETY OF TESTOSTERONE REPLACEMENT THERAPY IN MIDDLE-AGED AND OLDER MEN WITH HYPOGONADISM | Older Men with Age-Related Decline in Testosterone

items.subtopic: C | SAFETY OF TESTOSTERONE REPLACEMENT THERAPY IN MIDDLE-AGED AND OLDER MEN WITH HYPOGONADISM | Older Men with Age-Related Decline in Testosterone
items.title: Risks and Monitoring of Testosterone Therapy in Older Men
items.content: Testosterone therapy in older men can lead to adverse effects such as increased risk of venous thromboembolism, atrial fibrillation, and acute kidney injury. It is associated with a higher incidence of clinical bone fractures despite increasing bone mineral density. Baseline evaluation for prostate cancer risk should be conducted before initiating treatment.
keyPoints: Increased risk of venous thromboembolism, atrial fibrillation, and acute kidney injury with testosterone therapy.
Higher incidence of clinical bone fractures in the testosterone group.
Baseline evaluation for prostate cancer risk is recommended before starting therapy.
items.reference: Men s Health · C | SAFETY OF TESTOSTERONE REPLACEMENT THERAPY IN MIDDLE-AGED AND OLDER MEN WITH HYPOGONADISM | Older Men with Age-Related Decline in Testosterone

items.subtopic: AGE-RELATED CHANGES IN FECUNDITY
items.title: Impact of Advanced Paternal Age on Fertility and Offspring Health
items.content: Advanced paternal age is associated with reduced fertility due to lower sperm motility, morphology issues, and increased sperm tail defects. Additionally, it elevates the risk of germline mutations in specific genes leading to autosomal dominant diseases and increases the likelihood of neurodevelopmental disorders and cardiac malformations in offspring.
keyPoints: Men >50 years have lower sperm motility and morphology compared to men aged 21–25 years.
Increased paternal age is linked with germline mutations in FGFR2, FGFR3, RET genes.

Offspring risks include chondroplasia, Pfeiffer's syndrome, thanatophoric dysplasia, Crouzon's syndrome, Apert's syndrome, MEN 2A, MEN 2B, Klinefelter's syndrome, trisomy 13 and 18, schizophrenia, autism, bipolar disorders, ventricular septal defects, atrial septal defects, patent ductus arteriosus.
items.reference: Men s Health · AGE-RELATED CHANGES IN FECUNDITY

items.subtopic: AGE-RELATED CHANGES IN FECUNDITY
items.title: Trends in Postponement of Childbearing and Associated Risks
items.content: The median age at first childbirth is increasing globally due to various societal factors, leading to heightened risks of involuntary childlessness. This trend is compounded by the adverse effects of advanced maternal and paternal ages on fecundity and increased comorbidities that may indirectly impact fertility.
keyPoints: Postponement of childbirth increases risk of involuntary childlessness.
Advanced parental age affects fecundity adversely, especially when both parents are >40 years old.
Increased workforce participation, changing career expectations, and contraceptive availability contribute to delayed childbearing.
items.reference: Men s Health · AGE-RELATED CHANGES IN FECUNDITY

items.subtopic: MUSCLE DYSMORPHIA SYNDROME IN MEN—A FORM OF BODY IMAGE DISORDER
items.title: Characteristics and Risks of Muscle Dysmorphia in Men
items.content: Muscle dysmorphia is a body image disorder prevalent among men, characterized by an obsession with muscularity and leanness. It often leads to significant social and occupational impairment.
keyPoints: Men with muscle dysmorphia have a strong desire for increased muscularity and lean appearance.
They frequently engage in weightlifting and use performance-enhancing drugs (PEDs), particularly anabolic-androgenic steroids (AAS).
Higher rates of mood, anxiety disorders, and obsessive-compulsive behaviors are noted among these individuals.
items.reference: Men s Health · MUSCLE DYSMORPHIA SYNDROME IN MEN—A FORM OF BODY IMAGE DISORDER

items.subtopic: MUSCLE DYSMORPHIA SYNDROME IN MEN—A FORM OF BODY IMAGE DISORDER
items.title: Adverse Effects and Mortality Associated with AAS Abuse
items.content: The use of anabolic-androgenic steroids (AAS) among men, particularly those with muscle dysmorphia, is linked to severe health risks and increased mortality.
keyPoints: A Finnish study reported a 4.6 times higher risk of death in elite power lifters compared to age-matched general population males.
Causes of death include suicides (32%), homicides (26%), accidents (35%), myocardial infarction, hepatocellular failure, and non-Hodgkin's lymphoma.
Median age of death among AAS users is 24 years, lower than that for heroin or amphetamine users.
items.reference: Men s Health · MUSCLE DYSMORPHIA SYNDROME IN MEN—A FORM OF BODY IMAGE DISORDER

items.subtopic: MUSCLE DYSMORPHIA SYNDROME IN MEN—A FORM OF BODY IMAGE DISORDER
items.title: Cardiovascular Risks of High-Dose AAS Use
items.content: High doses of anabolic-androgenic steroids (AAS) are associated with significant cardiovascular risks, including proatherogenic dyslipidemia and increased thrombosis risk.
keyPoints: Reports indicate cardiac deaths among young AAS users due to adverse cardiovascular effects.
AAS may induce proatherogenic dyslipidemia and increase thrombosis risk by affecting clotting factors and platelets.
Vasospasm is another potential cardiovascular complication linked with high-dose AAS use.
items.reference: Men s Health · MUSCLE DYSMORPHIA SYNDROME IN MEN—A FORM OF BODY IMAGE DISORDER

items.subtopic: A. Four Major Categories of Serious Adverse Effects Associated with the Use of Anabolic-Androgenic Steroids
items.title: Cardiovascular Risks from AAS Use
items.content: Anabolic-androgenic steroids (AAS) use is linked to significant cardiovascular adverse effects, including hypertension, sudden death, cardiomyopathy, diastolic dysfunction, accelerated atherosclerosis, and dyslipidemia. These effects are partly due to the impact on vascular nitric oxide and induction of myocardial hypertrophy and fibrosis.
keyPoints: AAS use is associated with marked reductions in HDL cholesterol and increases in LDL cholesterol.
Studies report diastolic and systolic dysfunction, including lower early and late diastolic tissue velocities, reduced E/A ratio, and reduced peak systolic strain in AAS users compared to nonusers.
Power athletes using AAS often have short QT intervals but increased QT dispersion, predisposing them to ventricular arrhythmias.
Long-term AAS use may lead to myocardial hypertrophy and fibrosis, with tissue infiltration by fibrous tissue and fat droplets.
AAS users demonstrate higher coronary artery plaque volume than nonusers.
items.reference: Men s Health · A. Four Major Categories of Serious Adverse Effects Associated with the Use of Anabolic-Androgenic Steroids

items.subtopic: A. Four Major Categories of Serious Adverse Effects Associated with the Use of Anabolic-Androgenic Steroids
items.title: Endocrine Disruptions from AAS Withdrawal
items.content: Long-term use and subsequent withdrawal of anabolic-androgenic steroids can lead to significant endocrine disruptions, including suppression of the hypothalamic-pituitary-testicular axis, resulting in hypogonadism, infertility, testicular atrophy, and gynecomastia.
keyPoints: Long-term AAS use suppresses LH and FSH secretion, inhibiting endogenous testosterone production and spermatogenesis.

Men who have used AAS for more than a few months experience marked suppression of the HPT axis after stopping AAS, potentially causing sexual dysfunction, fatigue, infertility, and depressive symptoms.
In some long-term users, HPT suppression may last over a year with incomplete recovery in a few individuals.
As many as 30% of AAS users develop a syndrome of AAS dependence, characterized by continued use despite adverse effects.
Up to 25% of young men receiving testosterone replacement therapy in certain clinics have anabolic steroid withdrawal hypogonadism.
items.reference: Men s Health · A. Four Major Categories of Serious Adverse Effects Associated with the Use of Anabolic-Androgenic Steroids

items.subtopic: A. Four Major Categories of Serious Adverse Effects Associated with the Use of Anabolic-Androgenic Steroids
items.title: Neuropsychiatric and Cognitive Impacts of AAS
items.content: Anabolic-androgenic steroids can lead to major mood disorders, behavioral changes, dependence, addiction behaviors, and cognitive deficits. These effects include hypomania, mania during use, depression, suicidality during withdrawal, rage reactions, aggression, violence, and impaired memory and problem-solving.
keyPoints: AAS users may develop hypomanic and manic symptoms during exposure and major depression during withdrawal.
Users are at risk for developing other forms of illicit drug use.
AAS use is associated with difficulties in spatial and working memory, problem solving, attention, and changes in brain regions involved in inhibitory control and emotional regulation.
Structural MRI studies report smaller cortical, gray matter, putamen, and corpus callosum volumes in high-dose AAS users.
Both low and high androgen levels have been linked to increased amyloid-beta (Aβ) and tau-P levels and Aβ toxicity, raising concerns about the risk of Alzheimer's disease.
items.reference: Men s Health · A. Four Major Categories of Serious Adverse Effects Associated with the Use of Anabolic-Androgenic Steroids

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Detection of AAS Use | TABLE 411-5 Detection of the Use of Anabolic-Androgenic Steroids
items.title: Clinical Indicators for Suspecting AAS Use
items.content: Suspicion of anabolic-androgenic steroid (AAS) use should be raised by specific clinical and laboratory findings. Clinically, a 'V' muscular phenotype, reduced testicular volume (<15 mL), and excessive concern about leanness and muscularity are indicators. Laboratory findings include suppressed LH, FSH, SHBG levels, increased hematocrit, low HDL cholesterol, and low sperm density.
keyPoints: Clinical indicators: 'V' muscular phenotype, reduced testicular volume (<15 mL), excessive concern about leanness and muscularity
Laboratory indicators: Suppressed LH, FSH, SHBG levels; increased hematocrit
items.reference: Men s Health · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Detection of AAS Use | TABLE 411-5 Detection of the Use of Anabolic-Androgenic Steroids

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Detection of AAS Use | TABLE 411-5 Detection of the Use of Anabolic-Androgenic Steroids
items.title: Laboratory Methods for Detecting Exogenous Testosterone Use
items.content: Detection of exogenous testosterone use involves measuring the urinary testosterone-to-epitestosterone ratio and confirming with isotope ratio mass spectrometry. A ratio >4 suggests exogenous use but may also reflect genetic variations. Isotope ratio combustion mass spectrometry detects differences in the $^{13}C:^{12}C$ ratio, distinguishing synthetic from endogenously produced testosterone.
keyPoints: Urinary testosterone-to-epitestosterone ratio >4 suggests exogenous use
Isotope ratio mass spectrometry detects $^{13}C:^{12}C$ ratio differences to confirm exogenous testosterone
items.reference: Men s Health · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Detection of AAS Use | TABLE 411-5 Detection of the Use of Anabolic-Androgenic Steroids

items.subtopic: Integrated Management of Patients with AAS Use
items.title: Approach to Treating AAS Withdrawal Symptoms
items.content: Management of patients withdrawing from anabolic-androgenic steroids (AAS) involves addressing both the physiological symptoms and underlying psychological issues. This includes restoring testosterone levels using pharmacological agents such as clomiphene citrate, hCG, or aromatase inhibitors.
keyPoints: Clomiphene citrate can be administered in doses of 25–50 mg on alternate days to increase LH and FSH levels and restore testosterone levels in most men with AAS withdrawal syndrome.
hCG administered by intramuscular injections at 1500 to 2000 IU three times each week can raise testosterone levels into the normal range.
Recovery of sexual function during clomiphene administration is variable despite improvements in testosterone levels.
Some patients may not respond to either clomiphene or hCG therapy, indicating possible irreversible long-term toxic effects on Leydig cell function.
items.reference: Men s Health · Integrated Management of Patients with AAS Use

items.subtopic: Integrated Management of Patients with AAS Use
items.title: Psychological and Behavioral Interventions for AAS Users
items.content: Addressing the psychological aspects of AAS use is crucial, involving cognitive and behavioral therapy to manage body dysmorphia and addiction behaviors. Antidepressants may be necessary for treating depression associated with AAS withdrawal.
keyPoints: Cognitive and behavioral therapy are essential for managing muscle dysmorphia syndrome and addiction behaviors in AAS users.
Antidepressant therapy is recommended for treating depressive symptoms in patients withdrawing from AAS.
Naltrexone, an opioid antagonist, may block AAS dependence as it does in animal models, suggesting potential benefits in human AAS dependence treatment.
items.reference: Men s Health · Integrated Management of Patients with AAS Use

items.subtopic: LUTS IN MEN | Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms
items.title: Complex Etiology and Evaluation of LUTS in Men
items.content: Lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) in men are multifactorial, involving structural or functional abnormalities across multiple components of the lower urinary tract. The pathophysiology includes detrusor muscle overactivity, prostate enlargement, bladder neck issues, urethral problems, and neural control dysfunctions. Medications with anticholinergic properties can exacerbate LUTS.
keyPoints: LUTS are multifactorial: structural/functional abnormalities of the bladder, bladder neck, prostate, distal sphincter mechanism, urethra; neural control issues; autonomic dysfunction.
Medications with anticholinergic properties (e.g., diuretics, antihistamines, antidepressants) can cause or exacerbate LUTS in older men.
LUTS prevalence: ~50% of men >65 years old and 70% of men >80 years old.
items.reference: Men s Health · LUTS IN MEN | Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms

items.subtopic: LUTS IN MEN | Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms
items.title: Clinical Evaluation and Management Thresholds for LUTS in Men
items.content: Medical evaluation of LUTS should include a comprehensive assessment of potential causes, medication review, symptom severity using the International Prostate Symptom Score, and impact on quality of life. Digital prostate examination, neurologic examination, urinalysis, fasting blood glucose, electrolytes, creatinine, and PSA are part of the workup. Urodynamic studies are reserved for cases considering invasive surgical therapies.
keyPoints: Assessment includes: potential causes, medication review (including herbal/OTC), symptom severity using International Prostate Symptom Score, impact on sleep and daily living.
Evaluation involves: digital prostate examination, neurologic exam focused on perineum/lower extremities, urinalysis, fasting blood glucose, electrolytes, creatinine, PSA.
Urodynamic studies are recommended when considering invasive surgical therapies.
items.reference: Men s Health · LUTS IN MEN | Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms

items.subtopic: Patients with LUTS
items.title: Management Strategies for Mild to Moderately Severe LUTS in Men
items.content: In men presenting with mild to moderately severe lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS), initial management can focus on lifestyle modifications and pharmacotherapy. Lifestyle changes such as reducing caffeine and alcohol intake, adjusting diuretic medication timing, bladder training, pelvic floor exercises, and timed voiding regimens may alleviate symptoms. Pharmacological treatment includes α-adrenergic antagonists as first-line therapy, with options to add steroid 5α-reductase inhibitors or PDE5 inhibitors for additional symptom control.
keyPoints: Lifestyle modifications can be helpful in reducing LUTS severity.
Selective α-adrenergic antagonists are typically the first line of pharmacotherapy.
Side effects of α-adrenergic antagonists include hypotension, dizziness, nasal congestion, retrograde or delayed ejaculation, and rarely floppy iris syndrome.
Steroid 5α-reductase inhibitors (finasteride or dutasteride) improve urinary symptoms, flow rate, and reduce prostatic volume.
Combined therapy with a steroid 5α-reductase inhibitor and an α₁-adrenergic blocker can rapidly improve urinary symptoms.
items.reference: Men s Health · Patients with LUTS

items.subtopic: Patients with LUTS
items.title: Indications for Surgical Intervention in Men with LUTS
items.content: Surgical intervention is considered when medical management of lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) fails or if the patient develops complications. Indications include progression of symptoms despite therapy, acute urinary retention, hydronephrosis, renal insufficiency, recurrent urinary tract infections, and a postvoid residual urine volume greater than 25% of bladder capacity.
keyPoints: Surgery is indicated when medical therapy fails or symptoms progress.
Complications warranting surgery include acute urinary retention, hydronephrosis, renal insufficiency, and recurrent UTIs.
A postvoid residual urine volume >25% of the urinary bladder volume necessitates surgical evaluation.
items.reference: Men s Health · Patients with LUTS

items.subtopic: MEDICAL COMPLICATIONS OF PROSTATE CANCER THERAPY
items.title: Testosterone Deficiency Post-Prostate Cancer Therapy
items.content: Men treated for prostate cancer, especially those who have undergone radical prostatectomy or radiation therapy, often experience testosterone deficiency. This condition is associated with fatigue, sexual dysfunction, mobility limitation, and decreased physical function.
keyPoints: Testosterone levels are lower in men post-prostatectomy compared to noncancer age-matched controls.
40–50% of men undergoing radical prostatectomy report moderate to large problems with sexual performance 18 months after surgery.
items.reference: Men s Health · MEDICAL COMPLICATIONS OF PROSTATE CANCER THERAPY

items.subtopic: MEDICAL COMPLICATIONS OF PROSTATE CANCER THERAPY
items.title: Risks and Considerations for Testosterone Therapy in Prostate Cancer Survivors
items.content: Historically, a history of prostate cancer was considered a contraindication to testosterone therapy due to concerns about promoting metastatic growth. However, recent evidence suggests that men with organ-confined disease (pT2), Gleason score 6 or 7 (3+4), and undetectable PSA levels for >2 years post-prostatectomy may be considered for testosterone therapy on an individualized basis.
keyPoints: Men with a history of prostate cancer have low rates of PSA recurrence after radical prostatectomy when undergoing testosterone therapy.
Consider testosterone therapy if preoperative PSA <10 ng/mL, undetectable PSA (<0.1 ng/mL) for >2 years post-prostatectomy, and very low risk of disease recurrence (<0.5% at 10 years).
Careful monitoring of PSA levels is essential when instituting testosterone therapy.
items.reference: Men s Health · MEDICAL COMPLICATIONS OF PROSTATE CANCER THERAPY

items.subtopic: MEDICAL COMPLICATIONS OF PROSTATE CANCER THERAPY
items.title: Complications from Androgen Deprivation Therapy (ADT) in Advanced Prostate Cancer
items.content: Men with locally advanced or metastatic prostate cancer undergoing ADT experience significant side effects, including fatigue, sexual dysfunction, hot flushes, and increased risk for diabetes, metabolic syndrome, coronary heart disease, and frailty.
keyPoints: ADT leads to severe symptoms such as fatigue, sexual dysfunction, and hot flushes.
Increased risk of diabetes, metabolic syndrome, coronary heart disease, and frailty is associated with ADT.
items.reference: Men s Health · MEDICAL COMPLICATIONS OF PROSTATE CANCER THERAPY

items.subtopic: MEDICAL COMPLICATIONS OF ADT
items.title: Impact of ADT on Metabolic and Cardiovascular Health in Prostate Cancer Patients
items.content: Androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) is associated with significant metabolic changes, including insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia, leading to an increased risk of diabetes. Additionally, there is a notable prevalence of metabolic syndrome in men undergoing long-term ADT.
keyPoints: Metabolic syndrome is prevalent in >50% of men on long-term ADT compared to age-matched controls not on ADT.
ADT increases the risk of incident diabetes due to rapid induction of insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia.
Some studies report increased cardiovascular events, peripheral vascular disease, thromboembolic events, and cognitive dysfunction in patients receiving ADT.
items.reference: Men s Health · MEDICAL COMPLICATIONS OF ADT

items.subtopic: MEDICAL COMPLICATIONS OF ADT
items.title: Bone Health Risks Associated with ADT in Prostate Cancer Patients
items.content: ADT significantly increases the risk of osteoporosis and bone fractures due to profound hypogonadism, which leads to decreased muscle mass, increased fat mass, and bone loss.
keyPoints: Profound hypogonadism from ADT is linked with bone loss and increased fracture risk.
ADT is associated with a substantially increased risk of osteoporosis.
items.reference: Men s Health · MEDICAL COMPLICATIONS OF ADT

items.subtopic: Men Receiving ADT
items.title: Assessing Risks and Preventive Measures for Bone Loss in Men Undergoing ADT
items.content: When considering ADT for nonmetastatic prostate cancer, evaluate cardiovascular, diabetes, and fracture risks. Implement preventive measures against bone loss, such as physical activity, adequate calcium and vitamin D intake, and pharmacologic therapy for those with a history of minimal trauma fractures or a >20% 10-year risk of major osteoporotic fractures.
keyPoints: Assess cardiovascular, diabetes, and fracture risks before ADT.
Preventive measures include physical activity, calcium, vitamin D intake, and pharmacologic therapy for those with prior minimal trauma fractures or >20% 10-year risk of major osteoporotic fractures.
Bisphosphonates and denosumab reduce fracture risk; zoledronic acid and denosumab are FDA-approved to prevent metastasis-related skeletal events.
items.reference: Men s Health · Men Receiving ADT

items.subtopic: Men Receiving ADT
items.title: Managing Metabolic and Vasomotor Symptoms in Men on ADT
items.content: Monitor men receiving ADT for weight gain, diabetes, and other cardiometabolic complications. Encourage lifestyle interventions such as physical activity and smoking cessation. Use metformin with lifestyle changes to manage metabolic dysregulation. For hot flushes, consider gabapentin, medroxyprogesterone, or venlafaxine over placebo, while weighing potential side effects.
keyPoints: Monitor for weight gain, diabetes, and cardiometabolic risks during ADT.
Encourage lifestyle interventions: physical activity, exercise, dietary management, blood pressure control, lipid profile monitoring, and smoking cessation.
Metformin plus lifestyle intervention can prevent/improve metabolic dysregulation.
Gabapentin, medroxyprogesterone, and venlafaxine are effective for hot flushes; consider side effects like weight gain with medroxyprogesterone and dry mouth with venlafaxine.

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF SEXUALLY TRANSMITTED DISEASES
items.title: Importance of Structured Risk Assessment and Counseling in STI Prevention
items.content: A structured risk assessment is crucial for identifying individuals at increased risk for sexually transmitted infections (STIs). This includes adolescent boys, young men aged 15–24 years, men who have sex with men, those with multiple partners or unprotected sex, and those using illicit drugs. Counseling on safe sex practices, including consistent condom use, is essential to prevent STIs.
keyPoints: Men aged 15–24 are at increased risk for STIs.
STIs can lead to complications like cancers and infertility.
USPSTF recommends HIV screening for all men aged 15–65 years.
Behavioral counseling is recommended for sexually active adolescents and adult men at increased risk.
Condom use is the most important method of preventing STIs.

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF SEXUALLY TRANSMITTED DISEASES
items.title: Role of Immunization in Preventing STI-Related Complications
items.content: Immunizations play a critical role in preventing infections that can lead to STIs or their complications. The CDC recommends hepatitis B vaccination for unvaccinated adults, HIV-infected individuals, and health workers. HPV vaccination is advised for males aged 9–21 years and those with specific risk factors up to age 26. Meningococcal vaccination is recommended for men who have sex with men during outbreaks and all HIV-infected men.
keyPoints: Effective immunizations are available against hepatitis B, HPV, and Neisseria meningitides.
CDC recommends universal hepatitis B immunization for specific high-risk groups.
HPV vaccination is recommended for males aged 9–21 years and up to age 26 for those with certain risk factors.
Meningococcal vaccination is indicated during outbreaks and for all HIV-infected men.

items.subtopic: SEX DIFFERENCES IN COVID-19 DISEASE OUTCOMES
items.title: Why Men Have Worse COVID-19 Outcomes Than Women
items.content: Men infected with SARS-CoV-2 are more likely to experience severe disease, require mechanical ventilation, face complications, and die compared to women. This disparity is attributed to behavioral factors (e.g., higher smoking and alcohol use), biological factors (e.g., higher comorbid conditions in men), differences in immune responses (poorer T lymphocyte response in men), and genetic factors (lower expression of X-linked genes involved in viral detection). Additionally, hormonal regulation by androgens may increase susceptibility due to higher ACE2 and TMPRSS2 expression.
keyPoints: Men have higher rates of severe disease, mechanical ventilation needs, complications, and mortality from COVID-19 compared to women.
Behavioral factors: Higher smoking and alcohol use in men.
Biological factors: Higher comorbid conditions in men.
Immune response differences: Poorer T lymphocyte response in men.
Genetic factors: Lower expression of X-linked genes involved in viral detection in men.
Hormonal regulation: Androgens increase ACE2 and TMPRSS2 expression, potentially increasing susceptibility.
items.reference: Men s Health · SEX DIFFERENCES IN COVID-19 DISEASE OUTCOMES

items.subtopic: SEX DIFFERENCES IN COVID-19 DISEASE OUTCOMES
items.title: How Hormones Influence COVID-19 Susceptibility in Men
items.content: The regulation of ACE2 and TMPRSS2 expression by androgens in lung epithelial cells may contribute to men's increased susceptibility to SARS-CoV-2. Higher testosterone levels could lead to greater expression of these proteins, facilitating viral entry into alveolar cells.
keyPoints: ACE2 and TMPRSS2 are host proteins that facilitate SARS-CoV-2 entry into alveolar cells.
Expression of ACE2 and TMPRSS2 is regulated by androgens in lung epithelial cells.
Higher testosterone levels in men may increase susceptibility to COVID-19.
